content:
Think of a blockchain as a shared notebook that many people keep copies of, instead of one bank holding the official ledger. When someone makes a transaction, it’s written in everyone’s notebook—only after most agree the transaction is valid. This agreement happens through a process where computers check the details and confirm no one is cheating, like trying to spend the same money twice.

Because everyone has the same record, it’s very hard to change old transactions without everyone noticing. There’s no central bank or single authority controlling the ledger; instead, it’s maintained collectively by many independent participants. This system relies on transparency and math rather than trust in one institution. So while it may seem unusual, blockchain replaces a central middleman with a network of checks, making the record of transactions hard to alter or fake. It’s not perfect, but that’s the basic idea behind blockchain’s secure transaction record without a bank.
